annotations.label: Hyperbolic geometry

anchor.snippet: hyperbolic geometry (also called Lobachevskian geometry or Bolyai – Lobachevskian geometry ) is a non-Euclidean geometry

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the Poincare hyperbolic metric on the upper half-plane, but not an axiomatic non-Euclidean geometry called hyperbolic geometry.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic parallel postulate

anchor.snippet: The parallel postulate of Euclidean geometry is replaced with:

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration formalizing the hyperbolic parallel postulate.

annotations.label: At least two parallels through an external point

anchor.snippet: there are at least two distinct lines through P that do not intersect R

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for existence of multiple hyperbolic parallels through an external point.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic plane

anchor.snippet: The hyperbolic plane is a plane where every point is a saddle point

annotations.note: Mathlib has the upper half-plane model type, but I found no saddle-point or curvature definition of the hyperbolic plane.

annotations.label: Pseudospherical surface geometry

anchor.snippet: Hyperbolic plane geometry is also the geometry of pseudospherical surfaces

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration connecting hyperbolic plane geometry with pseudospherical surfaces.

annotations.label: Saddle surfaces have negative curvature

anchor.snippet: Saddle surfaces have negative Gaussian curvature in at least some regions

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for Gaussian curvature of saddle surfaces.

annotations.label: Hyperboloid model events

anchor.snippet: The hyperboloid model of hyperbolic geometry provides a representation of events one temporal unit into the future in Minkowski space

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for the hyperboloid model or its interpretation as Minkowski-space events.

annotations.label: Events correspond to rapidities

anchor.snippet: Each of these events corresponds to a rapidity in some direction

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration relating hyperboloid-model events to rapidities.

annotations.label: Parallel postulate is only axiomatic difference

anchor.snippet: the only axiomatic difference is the parallel postulate

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib axiomatization comparing Euclidean and hyperbolic geometry by only the parallel postulate.

annotations.label: Absolute geometry

anchor.snippet: When the parallel postulate is removed from Euclidean geometry the resulting geometry is absolute geometry

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration defining absolute geometry as Euclidean geometry without the parallel postulate.

annotations.label: Kinds of absolute geometry

anchor.snippet: There are two kinds of absolute geometry, Euclidean and hyperbolic

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib classification of absolute geometries into Euclidean and hyperbolic cases.

annotations.label: Absolute geometry theorems hold

anchor.snippet: All theorems of absolute geometry, including the first 28 propositions of book one of Euclid's Elements , are valid in Euclidean and hyperbolic geometry

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration formalizing this transfer of absolute-geometry theorems.

annotations.label: Euclid I.27-I.28 parallel lines

anchor.snippet: Propositions 27 and 28 of Book One of Euclid's Elements prove the existence of parallel/non-intersecting lines

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration corresponding to Euclid I.27 or I.28 as propositions about parallel lines.

annotations.label: Euclidean equivalences may fail hyperbolically

anchor.snippet: concepts that are equivalent in Euclidean geometry are not equivalent in hyperbolic geometry

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration comparing Euclidean equivalences with hyperbolic failures.

annotations.label: Absolute scale

anchor.snippet: hyperbolic geometry has an absolute scale , a relation between distance and angle measurements

annotations.note: The upper-half-plane distance is formalized with a fixed scale, but no declaration states the absolute-scale principle involving distances and angles.

annotations.label: Single-line properties match Euclidean geometry

anchor.snippet: Single lines in hyperbolic geometry have exactly the same properties as single straight lines in Euclidean geometry

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of hyperbolic lines supporting this comparison.

annotations.label: Two points define a line

anchor.snippet: two points uniquely define a line

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the affine line through two points and its minimality in affine geometry, but not for hyperbolic lines.

annotations.label: Line segments extend infinitely

anchor.snippet: line segments can be infinitely extended

annotations.note: Mathlib has affine line parameterizations over a scalar field, but no hyperbolic line-segment extension theorem.

annotations.label: Intersecting-line properties match Euclidean geometry

anchor.snippet: Two intersecting lines have the same properties as two intersecting lines in Euclidean geometry

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem comparing intersecting hyperbolic lines with Euclidean ones.

annotations.label: Distinct lines intersect at most once

anchor.snippet: two distinct lines can intersect in no more than one point

annotations.note: Mathlib proves injectivity of nondegenerate affine line parameterizations, but not the hyperbolic line-intersection statement.

annotations.label: Intersecting lines form equal opposite angles

anchor.snippet: intersecting lines form equal opposite angles

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for vertical opposite angles of hyperbolic intersecting lines.

annotations.label: Adjacent angles are supplementary

anchor.snippet: adjacent angles of intersecting lines are supplementary

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for supplementary adjacent angles of hyperbolic intersecting lines.

annotations.label: Many lines avoid two intersecting lines

anchor.snippet: given two intersecting lines there are infinitely many lines that do not intersect either of the given lines

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about infinitely many hyperbolic lines avoiding two intersecting lines.

annotations.label: Line properties are model-independent

anchor.snippet: These properties are all independent of the model used

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration proving model-independence of hyperbolic line properties.

annotations.label: Non-intersecting lines differ from Euclidean case

anchor.snippet: Non-intersecting lines in hyperbolic geometry also have properties that differ from non-intersecting lines in Euclidean geometry

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration comparing non-intersecting hyperbolic and Euclidean lines.

annotations.label: At least two non-intersecting lines

anchor.snippet: there are at least two distinct lines through P that do not intersect R

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem for two non-intersecting hyperbolic lines through an external point.

annotations.label: Infinitely many hyperbolic parallels

anchor.snippet: there are through P an infinite number of coplanar lines that do not intersect R

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem asserting infinitely many hyperbolic parallels through a point.

annotations.label: Two classes of non-intersecting lines

anchor.snippet: These non-intersecting lines are divided into two classes

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition classifying non-intersecting hyperbolic lines into limiting-parallel and ultraparallel classes.

annotations.label: Limiting parallels

anchor.snippet: Two of the lines ( x and y in the diagram) are limiting parallels

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of limiting parallel lines in hyperbolic geometry.

annotations.label: Ultraparallel lines

anchor.snippet: All other non-intersecting lines have a point of minimum distance and diverge from both sides of that point, and are called ultraparallel

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of ultraparallel hyperbolic lines or their minimum-distance property.

annotations.label: Parallel lines as limiting parallels

anchor.snippet: Some geometers simply use the phrase " parallel lines" to mean " limiting parallel lines"

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib convention defining hyperbolic parallel lines to mean limiting parallels.

annotations.label: Angle of parallelism

anchor.snippet: This angle depends only on the Gaussian curvature of the plane and the distance PB and is called the angle of parallelism

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration defining the hyperbolic angle of parallelism.

annotations.label: Ultraparallel theorem

anchor.snippet: there is a unique line in the hyperbolic plane that is perpendicular to each pair of ultraparallel lines

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about a unique common perpendicular to ultraparallel hyperbolic lines.

anchor.snippet: the circumference of a circle of radius r is greater than

anchor.snippet: Then the circumference of a circle of radius r is equal to:

anchor.snippet: And the area of the enclosed disk is:

annotations.label: Circumference-to-radius ratio

anchor.snippet: the ratio of a circle's circumference to its radius is always strictly greater than

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem comparing hyperbolic circle circumference-to-radius ratios with Euclidean ones.

annotations.label: Small-circle Euclidean limit

anchor.snippet: it can be made arbitrarily close by selecting a small enough circle

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem for the Euclidean small-radius limit of hyperbolic circles.

annotations.label: Circle geodesic curvature formula

anchor.snippet: If the Gaussian curvature of the plane is −1 then the geodesic curvature of a circle of radius r is:

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for geodesic curvature of hyperbolic circles.

annotations.label: No equidistant line

anchor.snippet: there is no line whose points are all equidistant from another line

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem saying there are no equidistant hyperbolic lines.

annotations.label: Hypercycle

anchor.snippet: the points that are all the same distance from a given line lie on a curve called a hypercycle

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of hypercycles in hyperbolic geometry.

annotations.label: Horocycle

anchor.snippet: Another special curve is the horocycle , whose normal radii ( perpendicular lines) are all limiting parallel to each other

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of horocycles in hyperbolic geometry.

annotations.label: Two horocycles through two points

anchor.snippet: Through every pair of points there are two horocycles

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem on existence of two horocycles through two points.

annotations.label: Horocycle centers for two points

anchor.snippet: The centres of the horocycles are the ideal points of the perpendicular bisector of the line-segment between them

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about centers of horocycles through two points.

annotations.label: Three-point curve classification

anchor.snippet: Given any three distinct points, they all lie on either a line, hypercycle, horocycle , or circle

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib classification of three points by hyperbolic line, hypercycle, horocycle, or circle.

annotations.label: Line-segment length

anchor.snippet: The length of a line-segment is the shortest length between two points

annotations.note: Mathlib defines hyperbolic distance on the upper half-plane, but not line-segment length as a minimizing geodesic length.

annotations.label: Hypercycle arc length bounds

anchor.snippet: The arc-length of a hypercycle connecting two points is longer than that of the line segment and shorter than that of the arc horocycle

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem comparing hypercycle, segment, and horocycle arc lengths.

annotations.label: Horocycle arc length equality and bounds

anchor.snippet: The lengths of the arcs of both horocycles connecting two points are equal

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about equality or bounds for horocycle arc lengths.

annotations.label: Horocycle geodesic curvature

anchor.snippet: the geodesic curvature of a horocycle is 1

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem for geodesic curvature of horocycles.

annotations.label: Hypercycle geodesic curvature

anchor.snippet: that of a hypercycle is between 0 and 1

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem for geodesic curvature bounds of hypercycles.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic triangle angle sum

anchor.snippet: the sum of the angles of a triangle is always strictly less than π radians (180°)

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem for the angle sum of hyperbolic triangles.

annotations.label: Triangle defect

anchor.snippet: The difference is called the defect

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of hyperbolic triangle defect.

anchor.snippet: the defect of a convex hyperbolic polygon with

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of defect for convex hyperbolic polygons.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic triangle area by defect

anchor.snippet: The area of a hyperbolic triangle is given by its defect in radians multiplied by R 2

anchor.snippet: which is also true for all convex hyperbolic polygons

annotations.label: Hyperbolic triangle area bound

anchor.snippet: all hyperbolic triangles have an area less than or equal to R 2 π

annotations.label: Ideal triangle maximal area

anchor.snippet: The area of a hyperbolic ideal triangle in which all three angles are 0° is equal to this maximum

anchor.snippet: each hyperbolic triangle has an incircle

annotations.label: No circumcircle for horocyclic or hypercyclic vertices

anchor.snippet: if all three of its vertices lie on a horocycle or hypercycle , then the triangle has no circumscribed circle

annotations.label: Hyperbolic AAA congruence

anchor.snippet: if two triangles are similar, they must be congruent

annotations.label: Beltrami-Klein ball model

anchor.snippet: For higher dimensions this model uses the interior of the unit ball

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of the higher-dimensional Beltrami-Klein ball model.

annotations.label: Beltrami-Klein higher-dimensional lines

anchor.snippet: the chords of this n -ball are the hyperbolic lines

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration identifying higher-dimensional Beltrami-Klein lines with ball chords.

annotations.label: Beltrami-Klein straight lines

anchor.snippet: This model has the advantage that lines are straight

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem stating that lines are straight in the Beltrami-Klein model.

annotations.label: Beltrami-Klein angle distortion

anchor.snippet: the disadvantage that angles are distorted

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about angle distortion in the Beltrami-Klein model.

annotations.label: Beltrami-Klein circles not circles

anchor.snippet: circles are not represented as circles

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about representation of circles in the Beltrami-Klein model.

annotations.label: Beltrami-Klein distance

anchor.snippet: The distance in this model is half the logarithm of the cross-ratio

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for Beltrami-Klein distance via logarithm of a cross-ratio.

annotations.label: Poincare disk model

anchor.snippet: The Poincaré disk model , also known as the conformal disk model, also employs the interior of the unit circle

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the complex unit disk, but not the Poincare disk as a hyperbolic metric model.

annotations.label: Poincare disk lines

anchor.snippet: lines are represented by arcs of circles that are orthogonal to the boundary circle, plus diameters of the boundary circle

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for Poincare-disk hyperbolic lines as orthogonal circle arcs and diameters.

annotations.label: Poincare disk conformal

anchor.snippet: This model preserves angles, and is thereby conformal

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem proving conformality of the Poincare disk model.

annotations.label: Poincare disk isometries

anchor.snippet: All isometries within this model are therefore Möbius transformations

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem classifying Poincare-disk isometries as Mobius transformations.

annotations.label: Poincare disk circles

anchor.snippet: Circles entirely within the disk remain circles

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about hyperbolic circles remaining Euclidean circles in the Poincare disk model.

annotations.label: Euclidean and hyperbolic circle centers differ

anchor.snippet: the Euclidean center of the circle is closer to the center of the disk than is the hyperbolic center of the circle

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem comparing Euclidean and hyperbolic centers of Poincare-disk circles.

annotations.label: Poincare disk horocycles

anchor.snippet: Horocycles are circles within the disk which are tangent to the boundary circle

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of Poincare-disk horocycles as boundary-tangent circles.

annotations.label: Poincare disk hypercycles

anchor.snippet: Hypercycles are open-ended chords and circular arcs within the disc that terminate on the boundary circle at non-orthogonal angles

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of Poincare-disk hypercycles as nonorthogonal boundary-terminating arcs.

annotations.label: Poincare half-plane model

anchor.snippet: The Poincaré half-plane model takes one-half of the Euclidean plane, bounded by a line B of the plane, to be a model of the hyperbolic plane

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the open complex upper half-plane and its hyperbolic metric, but not the full model statement with an arbitrary boundary line B.

annotations.label: Boundary line excluded

anchor.snippet: The line B is not included in the model

annotations.note: The condition 0 < z.im in UpperHalfPlane.im_pos excludes the real boundary line in the standard upper-half-plane realization.

annotations.label: Upper half-plane realization

anchor.snippet: the x-axis is taken as line B and the half plane is the upper half

annotations.note: UpperHalfPlane.upperHalfPlaneSet is exactly the subset of complex numbers with positive imaginary part, i.e. the upper half-plane above the real axis.

annotations.label: Half-plane hyperbolic lines

anchor.snippet: Hyperbolic lines are then either half-circles orthogonal to B or rays perpendicular to B

annotations.note: Mathlib proves some fixed-point loci in the upper half-plane are vertical lines or half-circles, but it does not define hyperbolic lines this way.

annotations.label: Ray interval length

anchor.snippet: The length of an interval on a ray is given by logarithmic measure

annotations.note: UpperHalfPlane.dist_of_re_eq states that points on the same vertical line have distance equal to the distance between logarithms of their imaginary parts.

annotations.label: Ray length homothety invariance

anchor.snippet: it is invariant under a homothetic transformation

annotations.note: UpperHalfPlane.isometry_pos_mul proves that positive real scaling is an isometry of the upper half-plane metric.

annotations.label: Half-plane conformal

anchor.snippet: this model preserves angles, and is thus conformal

annotations.note: Mathlib gives the upper half-plane a complex manifold structure, but I found no theorem proving conformality of the hyperbolic metric model.

annotations.label: Half-plane isometries

anchor.snippet: All isometries within this model are therefore Möbius transformations of the plane

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Mobius actions on the upper half-plane and proves key generators are isometries, but not that all half-plane isometries are Mobius transformations.

annotations.label: Half-plane as disk limit

anchor.snippet: The half-plane model is the limit of the Poincaré disk model

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem deriving the half-plane model as a limit of the Poincare disk model.

annotations.label: Hyperboloid model

anchor.snippet: The hyperboloid model or Lorentz model employs a 2-dimensional hyperboloid of revolution

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of the hyperboloid or Lorentz model of hyperbolic geometry.

annotations.label: Hyperboloid model in relativity

anchor.snippet: This model has direct application to special relativity

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration connecting the hyperboloid model to special relativity.

annotations.label: Minkowski 3-space spacetime model

anchor.snippet: Minkowski 3-space is a model for spacetime

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of Minkowski 3-space as a spacetime model.

annotations.label: Hyperboloid as observer events

anchor.snippet: One can take the hyperboloid to represent the events

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration interpreting a hyperboloid as observer events.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic distance as rapidity

anchor.snippet: The hyperbolic distance between two points on the hyperboloid can then be identified with the relative rapidity between the two corresponding observers

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem identifying hyperboloid-model distance with relative rapidity.

annotations.label: Hyperboloid model higher dimension

anchor.snippet: The model generalizes directly to an additional dimension

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem generalizing the hyperboloid model to higher dimensions.

annotations.label: Hemisphere model role

anchor.snippet: The hemisphere model is not often used as model by itself, but it functions as a useful tool for visualizing transformations between the other models

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of the hemisphere model or its role in visualizing transformations.

annotations.label: Hemisphere model domain

anchor.snippet: The hemisphere model uses the upper half of the unit sphere

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of the hemisphere model domain as the upper half of the unit sphere.

annotations.label: Hemisphere hyperbolic lines

anchor.snippet: The hyperbolic lines are half-circles orthogonal to the boundary of the hemisphere

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for hemisphere-model hyperbolic lines as boundary-orthogonal half-circles.

annotations.label: Hemisphere projections yield models

anchor.snippet: The hemisphere model is part of a Riemann sphere , and different projections give different models of the hyperbolic plane

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem deriving hyperbolic-plane models from projections of a hemisphere in the Riemann sphere.

annotations.label: All models same structure

anchor.snippet: All models essentially describe the same structure

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem proving equivalence of all named models of the hyperbolic plane.

annotations.label: Models as coordinate charts

anchor.snippet: they represent different coordinate charts laid down on the same metric space

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration identifying the common hyperbolic models as charts on one metric space.

annotations.label: Characteristic curvature of hyperbolic plane

anchor.snippet: The characteristic feature of the hyperbolic plane itself is that it has a constant negative Gaussian curvature

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of the hyperbolic plane by constant negative Gaussian curvature.

annotations.label: Geodesic invariance

anchor.snippet: The geodesics are similarly invariant

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem proving invariance of hyperbolic geodesics across model transformations.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic geometry via geodesics

anchor.snippet: Hyperbolic geometry is generally introduced in terms of the geodesics and their intersections on the hyperbolic plane

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of hyperbolic geometry in terms of geodesics and their intersections.

annotations.label: Non-isometric Euclidean embedding

anchor.snippet: we can always embed it in a Euclidean space of same dimension, but the embedding is clearly not isometric

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about non-isometric Euclidean embeddings of hyperbolic space in the same dimension.

annotations.label: Infinitely many charts

anchor.snippet: The hyperbolic space can be represented by infinitely many different charts

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem that hyperbolic space has infinitely many different model charts.

annotations.label: Model transformations

anchor.snippet: Since the four models describe the same metric space, each can be transformed into the other

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem giving transformations between the four common hyperbolic plane models.

annotations.label: Gans model

anchor.snippet: David Gans proposed a flattened hyperboloid model

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of the Gans flattened hyperboloid model.

annotations.label: Gans model as projection

anchor.snippet: It is an orthographic projection of the hyperboloid model onto the xy-plane

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem identifying the Gans model as an orthographic projection of the hyperboloid model.

annotations.label: Gans model uses entire plane

anchor.snippet: this model utilizes the entire Euclidean plane

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem that the Gans model uses the entire Euclidean plane.

annotations.label: Gans model lines

anchor.snippet: The lines in this model are represented as branches of a hyperbola

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration representing Gans-model hyperbolic lines as branches of hyperbolas.

annotations.label: Conformal square model

anchor.snippet: The conformal square model of the hyperbolic plane arises from using Schwarz–Christoffel mapping to convert the Poincaré disk into a square

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of the conformal square model via a Schwarz-Christoffel map.

annotations.label: Conformal square finite extent

anchor.snippet: This model has finite extent, like the Poincaré disk

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about finite extent of the conformal square model.

annotations.label: Conformal square points inside square

anchor.snippet: all of the points are inside a square

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration that conformal-square-model points lie inside a square.

annotations.label: Conformal square conformal

anchor.snippet: This model is conformal

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem proving conformality of the conformal square model.

annotations.label: Band model

anchor.snippet: The band model employs a portion of the Euclidean plane between two parallel lines

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of the band model of the hyperbolic plane.

annotations.label: Band model preserved distance line

anchor.snippet: Distance is preserved along one line through the middle of the band

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about distance preservation along the middle line of the band model.

annotations.label: Band model metric

anchor.snippet: the metric is given by

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for the band-model metric formula.

annotations.label: Isometry reflection decomposition

anchor.snippet: Every isometry ( transformation or motion ) of the hyperbolic plane to itself can be realized as the composition of at most three reflections

annotations.note: Mathlib has Euclidean reflection infrastructure, but I found no theorem decomposing hyperbolic-plane isometries into at most three reflections.

annotations.label: Higher-dimensional reflection decomposition

anchor.snippet: In n -dimensional hyperbolic space, up to n +1 reflections might be required

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem giving the n+1 reflection bound for hyperbolic n-space isometries.

annotations.label: Isometry classification

anchor.snippet: All isometries of the hyperbolic plane can be classified into these classes

annotations.note: Mathlib classifies 2 by 2 matrices into parabolic, hyperbolic, and elliptic cases for upper-half-plane actions, but not the article's full isometry list.

annotations.label: Identity isometry

anchor.snippet: the identity isometry – nothing moves

annotations.note: UpperHalfPlane.glScalar_smul proves scalar GL matrices act trivially on the upper half-plane, but it is not the full named identity-isometry classification item.

annotations.label: Inversion through a point

anchor.snippet: inversion through a point (half turn) – two reflections through mutually perpendicular lines passing through the given point

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of hyperbolic inversion through a point as a half-turn from two reflections.

annotations.label: Rotation around a normal point

anchor.snippet: rotation around a normal point – two reflections through lines passing through the given point

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the unique fixed point of an elliptic orientation-preserving upper-half-plane action, but not rotations as products of two reflections.

annotations.label: Horolation

anchor.snippet: "rotation" around an ideal point (horolation) – two reflections through lines leading to the ideal point

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of horolation or ideal-point rotations in hyperbolic geometry.

annotations.label: Translation along a line

anchor.snippet: translation along a straight line – two reflections through lines perpendicular to the given line

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the modular T powers act as real translations on the upper half-plane, but not the reflection decomposition of hyperbolic translations along a line.

annotations.label: Reflection through a line

anchor.snippet: reflection through a line – one reflection

annotations.note: Mathlib characterizes fixed-point loci of orientation-reversing upper-half-plane actions, but does not define reflection through a hyperbolic line as an isometry class.

annotations.label: Glide reflection

anchor.snippet: combined reflection through a line and translation along the same line

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of glide reflections in hyperbolic geometry.

annotations.label: Escher conformal disk model

anchor.snippet: Circle Limit III and Circle Limit IV illustrate the conformal disc model

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib example formalizing Escher's Circle Limit works as illustrations of the conformal disk model.

annotations.label: Circle Limit III white lines are hypercycles

anchor.snippet: The white lines in III are not quite geodesics (they are hypercycles )

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about the curves in Circle Limit III being hypercycles rather than geodesics.

annotations.label: Negative curvature visible by angle sums

anchor.snippet: It is also possible to see the negative curvature of the hyperbolic plane, through its effect on the sum of angles in triangles and squares

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem connecting visual angle sums in such tilings with negative curvature.

annotations.label: Circle Limit III vertex angles

anchor.snippet: in Circle Limit III every vertex belongs to three triangles and three squares

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib formalization of the combinatorics of Escher's Circle Limit III vertices.

annotations.label: Euclidean comparison angle sum

anchor.snippet: In the Euclidean plane, their angles would sum to 450°

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration formalizing this specific 450 degree comparison for the Escher tiling vertex.

annotations.label: Triangle angle sum from Escher tiling

anchor.snippet: the sum of angles of a triangle in the hyperbolic plane must be smaller than 180°

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib derivation of the hyperbolic triangle angle-sum bound from Escher tiling data.

annotations.label: Exponential growth

anchor.snippet: Another visible property is exponential growth

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem stating exponential growth for the hyperbolic plane in this context.

annotations.label: Fish count grows exponentially

anchor.snippet: the number of fishes within a distance of n from the center rises exponentially

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib formalization of exponential fish-count growth in Escher's tiling.

annotations.label: Ball area grows exponentially

anchor.snippet: the area of a ball of radius n must rise exponentially in n

annotations.note: Mathlib defines hyperbolic volume on the upper half-plane, but I found no theorem proving exponential ball-area growth.

annotations.label: Crocheted hyperbolic planes

anchor.snippet: The art of crochet has been used to demonstrate hyperbolic planes

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib example formalizing crocheted demonstrations of hyperbolic planes.

annotations.label: HyperRogue hyperbolic tilings

anchor.snippet: HyperRogue is a roguelike game set on various tilings of the hyperbolic plane

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib formalization of HyperRogue or its hyperbolic tilings.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic geometry in all dimensions

anchor.snippet: a hyperbolic geometry exists for every higher number of dimensions

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib family of hyperbolic geometries or hyperbolic spaces in all dimensions.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic space as symmetric space

anchor.snippet: Hyperbolic space of dimension n is a special case of a Riemannian symmetric space of noncompact type

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib definition of hyperbolic n-space as a Riemannian symmetric space of noncompact type.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic space quotient

anchor.snippet: as it is isomorphic to the quotient

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration identifying hyperbolic space with the relevant orthogonal-group quotient.

annotations.label: Orthogonal group action on Minkowski space

anchor.snippet: The orthogonal group O(1, n ) acts by norm-preserving transformations on Minkowski space R 1, n

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for O(1,n) acting on Minkowski space R^(1,n).

annotations.label: Orthogonal group transitive action

anchor.snippet: it acts transitively on the two-sheet hyperboloid of norm 1 vectors

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem for transitivity of O(1,n) on the two-sheet unit hyperboloid.

annotations.label: Timelike lines meet antipodal hyperboloid points

anchor.snippet: Timelike lines (i.e., those with positive-norm tangents) through the origin pass through antipodal points in the hyperboloid

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about timelike lines through the origin meeting antipodal hyperboloid points.

annotations.label: Line-space model of hyperbolic n-space

anchor.snippet: the space of such lines yields a model of hyperbolic n -space

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib construction of hyperbolic n-space as a space of timelike lines.

annotations.label: Stabilizer of a line

anchor.snippet: The stabilizer of any particular line is isomorphic to the product of the orthogonal groups O( n ) and O(1)

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem identifying a timelike-line stabilizer with O(n) x O(1).

annotations.label: Linear-algebraic hyperbolic concepts

anchor.snippet: Many of the elementary concepts in hyperbolic geometry can be described in linear algebraic terms

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib development giving elementary hyperbolic concepts in this linear-algebraic homogeneous model.

annotations.label: Geodesic paths as intersections

anchor.snippet: geodesic paths are described by intersections with planes through the origin

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration describing hyperboloid-model geodesics as plane intersections.

annotations.label: Dihedral angles by inner products

anchor.snippet: dihedral angles between hyperplanes can be described by inner products of normal vectors

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib declaration for hyperbolic dihedral angles via Minkowski inner products of normal vectors.

annotations.label: Reflection groups by matrices

anchor.snippet: hyperbolic reflection groups can be given explicit matrix realizations

annotations.note: Mathlib has general linear reflection infrastructure, but I found no hyperbolic reflection groups realized by explicit matrices.

annotations.label: Small-dimensional exceptional isomorphisms

anchor.snippet: In small dimensions, there are exceptional isomorphisms of Lie groups that yield additional ways to consider symmetries of hyperbolic spaces

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem formalizing these exceptional Lie-group isomorphisms for hyperbolic-space symmetries.

annotations.label: Dimension two Lie group isomorphisms

anchor.snippet: in dimension 2, the isomorphisms SO + (1, 2) ≅ PSL(2, R ) ≅ PSU(1, 1) allow one to interpret the upper half plane model as the quotient SL(2, R )/SO(2) and the Poincaré disc model as the quotient SU(1, 1)/U(1)

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem for SO+(1,2), PSL(2,R), and PSU(1,1) isomorphisms or the stated quotient models.

annotations.label: Symmetry groups act fractionally linearly

anchor.snippet: the symmetry groups act by fractional linear transformations

annotations.note: UpperHalfPlane.coe_smul gives the fractional-linear formula for the GL(2,R) action on the upper half-plane, but not the full symmetry-group statement across models.

annotations.label: Cayley transformation conjugates symmetry groups

anchor.snippet: The Cayley transformation not only takes one model of the hyperbolic plane to the other, but realizes the isomorphism of symmetry groups as conjugation in a larger group

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem about the Cayley transform conjugating hyperbolic-plane symmetry groups.

annotations.label: Dimension three boundary action

anchor.snippet: In dimension 3, the fractional linear action of PGL(2, C ) on the Riemann sphere is identified with the action on the conformal boundary of hyperbolic 3-space

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib theorem identifying the PGL(2,C) Riemann-sphere action with the conformal boundary action of hyperbolic 3-space.

annotations.label: Studying hyperbolic 3-isometries by matrices

anchor.snippet: This allows one to study isometries of hyperbolic 3-space by considering spectral properties of representative complex matrices

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib development of hyperbolic 3-space isometries via spectra of complex matrices.

annotations.label: Parabolic transformations

anchor.snippet: parabolic transformations are conjugate to rigid translations in the upper half-space model

annotations.note: Mathlib defines parabolic GL(2) elements and related upper-triangular characterizations, but I found no theorem stating conjugacy to rigid translations in upper half-space.

annotations.label: Unipotent triangular representatives

anchor.snippet: they are exactly those transformations that can be represented by unipotent upper triangular matrices

annotations.note: Mathlib proves a determinant ±1 upper-triangular parabolic iff it is represented by upperRightHom or its negative, a special case of the unipotent triangular representative claim.
